title: Cedar Fire - Idaho
field_incident_description: The Cedar Fire is being managed using a combination of containment and confinement strategies. Firefighter safety is always the number one priority, and the confinement strategy was chosen due to the rugged conditions and steep slopes in the Lemhi Range. Firefighters have completed a majority of the containment firelines, in areas safe for them to work. The remaining areas contain natural barriers such as steep rock faces and scree slopes that will confine the fire to the current perimeter.

The Kinkaid Creek, South Nanamkin, and North Gua Springs, fires are being managed by NW Team 3 Complex Incident Management Team.

Incident Information below is for the Kinkaid Creek fire. The Bear Creek II incident and nearby Anderson Creek fire are also being managed by NW Team 3.

title: Middle Mesa Fire
field_incident_overview:
The Middle Mesa Fire is a lightning-caused wildfire on the Jicarilla Ranger District of the Carson National Forest. Smoke has been visible to nearby areas, including Navajo Lake and Arboles, CO.

Ground and air resources are working to suppress the fire, which is burning in a remote area where natural gas infrastructure, such as wells and pipelines, is located.

Fire managers request that the public avoid the northern reaches of the ranger district for safety of themselves and fire crews.

The Rio Arriba County Sheriff's Office has placed a "Set" evacuation status on private inholdings and adjacent properties within the county that are the path of the fire.

No Drones: If you fly, we can't! Keep drones grounded for the safety of firefigthing aircraft personnel.

Take Action

  • Stay informed and subscribe to the Carson National Forest email list to get updates about this wildfire and more.
  • Are you and your home prepared for wildfire? Use New Mexico's guide to get ready.

title: Stud Horse Fire
field_incident_description: Lightning caused wildfire, threating vaules at risk, including homes and recreational lands.
field_incident_overview:
The Stud Horse Fire, located approximately two miles east of Winthrop, WA, was discovered on Thursday July 31, 2025, at around 3:18 p.m. The fire spread very rapidly resulting in Okanogan County issuing evacuation notices for nearby areas. The fire held its footprint overnight, but numerous firefighters, engines, and equipment are still working to contain and fully extinguish the fire.

title: Gifford Fire
field_incident_overview:
UPDATES: Morning Update  | ACTUALIZACIÓN DE INCIDENTE  |  Operational Update Video  |  Vídeo Operativo (Español)   | Fire Behavior Video

MAPS: Public Information Map

CAMERA VIEWS: Plowshare Peak   |  La Panza 2

AIR QUALITY: AirNow.gov  | Santa Barbara Air Pollution Control District   |

 

COMMUNITY MEETING: Gifford Fire Officials will host a community meeting in Arroyo Grande Friday, August 8th from 7 to 8 PM to provide an update on the current situation and address questions. The meeting will be held at the Branch Elementary School (970 School Rd., Arroyo Grande, CA. 93420). 

Recording of community meeting that occurred today on August 7, 2025.

 

INITIAL SITUATION:  Los Padres National Forest firefighters responded to reports of multiple wildfires along Highway 166, west of Cuyama, in the afternoon of Friday, August 1st, 2025. Evacuations are in progress and road closures are now in effect. Los Padres National Forest is currently in unified command with Santa Barbara County Fire. A complex Incident Management Team has been ordered and will in-brief on the evening of Saturday, August 2nd, 2025.

 

CURRENT SITUATION: Overnight, the fire showed minimal growth following yesterday’s rapid expansion on the northwest corner, where it had pushed toward the Garcia Wilderness. A temperature inversion settled in, helping to suppress fire activity across much of the incident. This inversion acted like a lid, trapping smoke and cooler air near the ground. By holding in moisture and reducing wind, it created more favorable conditions for suppression efforts. However, in the upper elevations along Pine Ridge, above the inversion layer, where conditions remained warmer and drier, the fire was active.

Today, crews will continue building primary and secondary control lines between the power line corridor and Pine Ridge to contain the new growth that occurred on the northwest corner of the fire. Elsewhere, on the east and west sides of the fire, crews will continue mop-up and patrol operations, with most of the line now holding from the Madre burn scar on the west side and the containment lines on the east. In the south, in the San Rafael Wilderness, crews are working to secure the fire’s edge by constructing handline to connect with existing control lines.

 

EVACUATIONS: Due to the erratic fire behavior, residents should watch for changing conditions in their area. For information on evacuations in Santa Barbara County, please visit the following websites  ReadySBC.org |  Sign up for Ready SBC Alerts  | Gifford Fire Evacuation Map

    • For updates, please visit  Caltrans

Avoid traveling in this area, unless you are evacuating the area. 

 

FOREST AREA CLOSURE: The Los Padres National Forest has implemented a forest order for the Gifford Fire area,  this is due to provide for public safety. Please see Forest Order 05-07-53-25-13.  For more information on closures or recreation please visit: Los Padres National Forest website

 

KNOW WHERE YOU CAN FLY: Drones pose a serious risk to firefighting and can cause air operations to cease. When drones interfere with firefighting efforts, a wildfire has the potential to grow larger and cause more damage. A FAA Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) is in effect and any private aircraft or drone that violates the TFR could face serious criminal charges. For more information on drones the public can visit the FAA’s website at www.KnowBeforeYouFly.org “If you fly, we can’t!”

title: Castle Rock Fire
field_incident_description:
Castle Rock Fire Update
August 6, 2025

Quick Facts
Size: 58 acres Start Date: July 31, 2025 Cause: Lightning
Location: 2 Miles east of Arden
Containment: 30% (+5) Personnel: 150 (-12) Fire Strategy: Full suppression

The Castle Rock Fire is burning on Castle Rock Mountain approximately 2 miles east of Arden. The fire was lightning caused and quickly advanced to the northwest in thick vegetation and rugged terrain.

Today, grid and mop up operations continued throughout the fire as crews worked to identify isolated hotspots and mitigate remaining hazards. On the southeast flank, crews have been working meticulously to grid 100-feet from the fire perimeter working towards the interior. Crews working this flank have reported significant progress in these operations and are confident operations will be completed by tomorrow evening, pending PalmIR. On the Northeast flank, crews have completed a 150-foot grid, with a remaining 50 feet remaining. The team is confident this flank will be completed within the next two operational periods.

Weather remains a concern in and around the fire zone as high winds, hail, and thunderstorms are expected over the region in the next 24-48 hours. If the weather forecast holds, lightning safety protocols will be in effect. This may include a temporary operational safety standdown removing crews from the affected areas.

Northeast Washington Interagency Type 3 Incident Management Team #1 assumed command of the fire as of noon on 8/1/25.

title: Kirkland Fire
field_incident_description: Wildfire
field_incident_overview:
Kirkland Fire Update as of Thursday, August 7, at 630 pm:

Today the Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management's internal Type 3 team transitioned the fire to a Type 4 incident. A Type 4 incident is of a lower complexity. 

The fire has had no new growth over the last few days and continues to remain in its footprint. It is now 80% contained. One hand crew and three engines remain assigned to the incident and will continue to improve and monitor established containment lines, and patrol and monitor the fire's edge checking for remaining heat and interior smokes. As hot and dry conditions linger resources remain vigilant on the northwest and west flanks of the fire where open line remains. 

Hot and very dry conditions continue to hover across the fire area - and across the state. These weather conditions along with low relative humidity levels, wind gusts, and dry fuel could spur new starts in the area. Residents are urged to use caution when working outdoors or recreating. 

The Kirkland Fire started Friday, August 1, approximately 3:15 pm, 2 miles northeast of Hillside in Yavapai County. Due to hot and dry conditions, and driven by the wind, the fire quickly grew, pushing to the north/northeast toward the community of Yava.

title: Jakes Fire
field_incident_overview:
Note: The Jakes Fire, Snowstorm Fire, MP 22 Fire have merged and are now being reported as one incident, the Jakes Fire.

 

August 8, 2025 Update

Active fire behavior continues, with uphill runs, backing, and flanking. Elevated fire conditions are expected to persist today, with shifting winds of 10–20 mph and gusts up to 30 mph. Increased activity is anticipated on the southwest flank, where above-average fine fuel loading in drainages could contribute to rapid fire spread.

Resources will remain concentrated on direct attack strategies along the southwest flank. Six hand crews are spiked out in the Snowstorm Mountains and will continue building direct line, supported by heavy equipment in accessible terrain. Air resources will play a critical role in supporting these efforts.

On the eastern flank, crews and engines will continue to secure the perimeter. Current resources assigned include approximately 8 helicopters, 16 dozers, and 65 engines.

title: Lightning Creek Fire
field_incident_overview: The Lightning Creek Fire was ignited by lightning on 7/30/2025. It is burning up the Lightning Creek Drainage in the 2018 Cougar Fire scar.

title: Adobe Mountain Fire
field_incident_overview:
The Adobe Mtn Fire is burning 18 miles south of Wild Horse, NV.

 

August 8, 2025 Update

Containment has increased 22% since yesterday’s report. Fire behavior remains minimal, and the fire is expected to stay within its current footprint. Control lines have successfully held through two days of sustained gusty, erratic winds, dry conditions, and high temperatures.

Today, firefighters will continue reinforcing line around the perimeter and mopping up, focusing on extinguishing remaining heat along the northwest side.

title: White Sage Fire Burned Area Emergency Response (BAER)
field_incident_overview:
The White Sage Fire was started by lightning on July 9, 2025, near the community of White Sage approximately 15 miles southeast of Fredonia, Arizona, on the Bureau of Land Management’s Arizona Strip District. The wind-driven fire quickly grew onto the North Kaibab Ranger District despite the efforts of wildland firefighters who were able to respond a short time after the fire was first detected.

Driven by hot, dry, windy weather, the fire quickly grew and crossed onto the North Kaibab Ranger District by July 10. Command of the fire transferred command to the Color Country Team 2 Incident Management Team July 10 and was transferred on July 13 to Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 6. 

A Burned Area Emergency Response (BAER) Team was ordered to assess the effects of the White Sage Fire to critical Forest Service values within the fire perimeter. The Team, led by BAER Team Leader Paul Brown, began assessing the Fire on August 1.
field_title_and_unit: AZKNF White Sage Fire Burned Area Emergency Response BAER

title: Island Creek
field_incident_overview: The Island Creek fire was detected the evening of July 31, 2025. The fire is burning in steep terrain on the Moose Creek Ranger District near Sob Point, approximately 14 air miles north of Elk City, ID. It is approximately 11 air miles southeast of Lowell, ID.

title: Leroux Fire
field_incident_overview:
At approximately 2:30 PM on Sunday, August 3rd, a smoke report was called in to Delta Dispatch in the area of Oak Mesa outside of Hotchkiss, Colorado. A small, single tree fire was discovered by the local fire agency in steep, remote terrain with dangerous conditions for engagement. It was determined to be on BLM land and command was handed over to the agency. 

Fire behavior was extreme through the morning of August 4th and evacuations were ordered by Delta County Sheriff's Office at approximately 2:30 AM in the area along 3100 Road north of the Redlands Mesa Road intersection. 

Team Colorado, a State Type 3 IMT, took command of the fire on Tuesday, August 5 at 6 AM. 

See https://experience.arcgis.com/experience/d27b59bc525742278f92a60d6da278e5/#data_s=id%3AdataSource_1-19875ebdfd2-layer-10%3A1 for an interactive property map showing which addresses are in pre-evacuation status.

Visit the Information tab for additional fire updates.

Command of the Leroux Fire will be transferred back to the BLM Southwest District Fire Management Unit at 6:30 AM on Saturday, August 9.

title: Rosa Fire
field_incident_overview:
Rosa Fire Most Recent Update          Rosa Fire Map          Facebook:@SanBernardinoNF

The overnight humidity remained poor, leaving fuels dry and ready to burn. Hot and dry conditions are expected to continue. The fire remains within the established containment lines. Pockets of unburned fuels may burn within the interior portions of the fire. Please drive carefully as emergency personnel are still working throughout the fire area.For additional information when returning home after

The fire remains 1,690 acres and is now 40% contained.  The fire has burned along both sides of Highway 74, and is threatening the rural communities of Springcrest, Alpine Village, Pinyon and Pinyon Crest.   

1,081 fire fighters and support personnel are assigned to the incident, including a multitude of engines, water tenders, hand crews, and dozers. Fixed wing tankers have been released from the fire however two helicopters remain working with crews and engines to extinguish hot spots.   A mixed agency Type 3 Incident Management Team is managing this fire.

Closures:  

Highway 74 is closed between the Santa Rosa Pit Stop Gas Station and Palm Canyon Drive.  Through traffic is not being allowed at this time.  Please check for the latest updates at https://roads.dot.ca.gov/?roadnumber=74 

With Highway 74 reopened with access from Palm Desert, the Pinyon Campground, Cahuilla Tewanet Viewpoint, and the Sawmill and Cactus Springs Trail have reopened for public use.   The Santa Rosa Truck Trail and associated remote "yellow post" campsites, Upper Palm Canyon Trail, and the Pinyon/Omstott Trails will remain closed.

Evacuation Orders:  

Effective 8/8/25 @ 11:00AM; all residential evacuation orders are reduced to warnings.  A zoomable map with current evacuation status is available at https://protect.genasys.com/location?z=10.598512677515199&latlon=33.58898584640406%2C-116.4900073164431 

Returning Home:  https://readyforwildfire.org/post-wildfire/ 

Care and Reception Centers:  The following care & reception center will be closing:

Anza Community Center - 56630 CA-371, Anza, CA.

Palm Desert Community Center: 43900 San Pablo Avenue, Palm Desert, CA.

The following animal evacuation shelters are open:

San Jacinto Animal Shelter - 581 S. Grand Avenue, San Jacinto, CA.

Coachella Valley Animal Campus - 72050 Pet Land Place, Thousand Palms, CA.

Alerts are also being sent out via text message and landline phone systems.  If you have not signed up for this "Reverse 911" system, please take the time to do so.  

Information for Residents:

From the Anza Electric Coop - Electric service remains off in the Rosa fire area. We are working with CAL FIRE officials to gain access to assess damage and begin repairs. Due to the difficult terrain in many locations, these repairs may take a considerable amount of time. We intend to install temporary generation in the Pinyon area to feed the system, when and where it is safe to do so.

title: 2025 BDF Gold Fire
field_incident_description: Wildfire
field_incident_overview:
INITIAL SITUATION: San Bernardino National Forest firefighters responded to reports of a wildfire one mile NE of Holcomb Valley Campground in Big Bear, in the morning of Monday, August 4th, 2025. Holcomb Valley Campground was evacuated and remains closed.  Local Forest road closures are now in effect. San Bernardino National Forest is currently in unified command with CAL FIRE, BDU.

 

CURRENT SITUATION: The fire is being managed under Unified Command with San Bernardino National Forest and CAL FIRE San Bernardino Unit. Management of the fire will transition to California Incident Management Team 6 this evening. Fire progression slowed allowing firefighters to take more direct action. Ground Crews and aircraft will focus their efforts on the northeastern side building handline and using dozers where possible. The terrain in this area is steep and rugged with loose, difficult footing. Firefighters will work effectively taking appropriate action providing for personnel safety.

 

AREA CLOSURES: The following recreation sites and Forest Service roads are temporarily closed for fire personnel and public safety.

Recreation Sites: 

  • Holcomb Valley Campground
  • Pinnacles Climbing Area 

Forest Service Roads: 

  • 3N10 and all spur roads,
  • 3N07 and all spur roads,
  • 3N32
  • 3N43

 

KNOW WHERE YOU CAN FLY: Drones pose a serious risk to firefighting and can cause air operations to cease. When drones interfere with firefighting efforts, a wildfire has the potential to grow larger and cause more damage. A FAA Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) is in effect and any private aircraft or drone that violates the TFR could face serious criminal charges. For more information on drones the public can visit the FAA’s website at www.KnowBeforeYouFly.org “If you fly, we can’t!”

title: Elk RBX Fire
field_incident_overview: Lightning storm went through a few days prior to the start of the Elk and Lee Fires. The Elk fire was discovered on Saturday, August 2nd as a lightning strike on private land in the Oak Ridge area. Drought with severe and extreme weather conditions along with two days of red flag warning caused heightened fire activity.

title: Stoner Mesa Fire
field_incident_description:
The fire is located in a remote, extremely difficult to access area of the San Juan National Forest, on Stoner Mesa.
The fire is is growing to the east. It has not reached Stoner Creek at this time.
Dolores County has issued an evacuation order for San Juan National Forest lands on Stoner and Taylor Mesa. This includes Stoner Mesa Road (FS Road 686), Taylor Mesa Road (FS Road 545) as well as all roads branching off, Mavreeso Campground, and West Dolores Campground. Dolores and Montezuma County Sheriffs Offices are coordinating this evacuation.
Aerial resources, including multiple air tankers dropping retardant, are being used. Fire managers are assessing where and how to insert firefighters in order to safely and effectively work to suppress the fire.
field_incident_overview:
The Stoner Mesa Fire is burning in mixed conifer with substantial dead standing and down trees. Fire behavior has been extreme in the afternoon with group tree torching and spotting ahead of the main fire. Crews, including the San Juan Hotshots, are constructing fireline along the FS Road 555 spur. This line will be comprised of roads, dozer line, and handline. Fire managers will continue scouting the fire area for potential control features including roads, trails, and natural barriers such as rock outcroppings and creeks. This work is part of an indirect suppression strategy, establishing fireline as close as possible to the fire while maintaining firefighter safety and improving the long-term probability of success. 

A Fire Area Closure Order is in effect for the Stoner Mesa Fire Area, Road and Trail Closure #Order No.: 02-13-05-25-14; This Order shall be in effect from August 6, 2025, at 12:00 a.m. through December 31, 2025, at 12:00 a.m., unless rescinded.

There are no anticipated closures to Hwy 145 and CR 38. Any future evacuations would be announced by Dolores County Emergency Alerts.

title: Lee Fire
field_incident_overview: The Lee and Grease fires were discovered on August 2nd as a lightning strikes on Bureau of Land Management land in the Piceance area. The Lee and Grease fires quickly met to be known exclusively as the Lee fire. Drought with severe and extreme weather conditions along with two days of red flag warning caused heightened fire activity.

title: Silver Mountain Fire
field_incident_overview:
At approximately 6:30 p.m. on Tuesday, Aug. 5, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance on a fire burning south of San Antonio, in Bexar County, Texas. The Silver Mountain Fire is burning in brush and grass vegetation. The fire is exhibiting active fire behavior with some crowning and spotting.

Texas A&M Forest Service is working in unified command with local responders. Local aircraft are assisting crews on the ground by making strategic drops of water to slow the flanks and head of the fire.

title: Gill Gulch Fire
field_incident_overview:
The Gill Gulch Fire was reported August 4th and is located approximately seven miles south of Lincoln, MT.  Yesterday, firefighters and heavy equipment were able to establish an anchor point and complete suppression line on the west flank of the fire.  Today, firefighters and heavy equipment will continue to work on suppression line around the perimeter of the fire.  Crews will also continue working on contingency lines and structure protection.  Chipping operations to remove woody materials will be done along Fields Gulch Road and Stemple Pass Road.  Rain was received over the fire yesterday. Cooler temperatures, elevated humidity, and a slight chance of showers are expected over the fire today. 

There are no area closures in place for the Gill Gulch Fire.
